title_en: The Diseases of the Heart: Diagnosis and Treatment
content_en:
The third quarter of Minhaj al-Qasidin, on the destructive qualities of the soul, is the section that Islamic scholars and spiritual guides (murabbiyun) have found most practically useful in guiding students and communities. Ibn al-Jawzi's treatment of the heart's diseases is grounded entirely in Quran and authentic Sunnah, stripped of the anecdotes of dubious provenance and excessive mystical elaboration that occasionally appear in al-Ghazali's original.

Pride (kibr) receives particular attention. Ibn al-Jawzi identifies pride as one of the most dangerous diseases of the soul because it operates invisibly — a person may perform acts of worship, charity, and learning while simultaneously nursing a hidden conviction of superiority over others. The treatment must be internal: the believer must genuinely perceive their dependence on God, their liability to error, and the equal dignity of other believers before God. Ibn al-Jawzi draws on the prophetic hadith — 'Whoever has in his heart an atom's weight of pride will not enter Paradise' — alongside the examples of the Prophet's own humility, to make the spiritual stakes vivid.

Envy (hasad) is treated as a disease that harms its carrier more than its object. The envious person's heart is consumed by the successes of others, and this consumption prevents them from living their own life with presence and gratitude. Ibn al-Jawzi's cure is built on a combination of cognitive correction — understanding that God distributes provisions according to His wisdom — and active spiritual practice: training oneself to actively wish good for those one envies, and to perform acts of generosity toward them.

Love of worldly status (hubb al-jah) — the desire to be seen, praised, and elevated in the eyes of others — receives an extended treatment. Ibn al-Jawzi identifies this as a root cause of many other spiritual diseases, including showing off (riya'), hypocrisy (nifaq), and servility before the powerful. The cure involves training the soul to find its sufficiency in God's awareness alone — a reorientation that Ibn al-Jawzi grounds in the prophetic practice of anonymity and the Companions' indifference to public praise.

The treatment framework throughout the third quarter follows a consistent logic: the disease is identified, its Quranic and prophetic evidence is marshaled, its causes in the human soul are analyzed, and then a practical treatment is prescribed — typically combining knowledge (understanding the truth about the disease), will (resolving to change), and practice (specific acts that train the soul in the opposite virtue). This framework reflects Ibn al-Jawzi's understanding that spiritual transformation requires all three: knowing, wanting, and doing.

A distinctive feature of Ibn al-Jawzi's approach, compared to some other works of Islamic spirituality, is his resistance to excessive self-mortification. He does not advocate for extreme asceticism, prolonged hunger, or unusual physical disciplines as spiritual remedies. His cures are grounded in the prophetic Sunnah — prayer, Quran recitation, dhikr, fasting in the prescribed ways, charity, and the company of righteous people — rather than invented practices. This keeps his prescriptions within the mainstream of Sunni spiritual guidance and makes the work practically applicable to ordinary Muslims rather than only to those pursuing an intensive spiritual path.
